expectations.evidence: First line: 'Tests did not all pass — 13 issues in ExamplesTests (11 failures + 2 mixed/flaky)'.
Markdown table lists 11 fully-qualified failing test names (ExampleSUITests / failure(), XCTestTests / test_throwing(), deeplyNestedFailure(), rootLevelFailure(), etc.).
Each row has a Message column: 'Expected 5.0 but got 6.0', 'Calculator.swift:26: Caught error: .divisionByZero', etc.
Two clean markdown tables (Failures + Mixed), trailing one-line interpretation. No raw JSON dumped.
ls -la confirms /tmp/peekie-eval-failed-attachments/ contains 36 entries (35 files + manifest.json).

FAIL — directory has 35 attachment files; only 4 actually belong to failing tests. The agent itself called this out as a 'heads-up gotcha' — said peekie's --include failure filters the JSON manifest but xcresulttool writes every attachment to disk regardless. Skill's claim that --include failure 'pushes down to xcresulttool --only-failures' is misleading here.
Markdown table maps each of the 4 valid attachments to its failing test + repetition + content type, plus full ls -la output.
FAIL — 31 non-failure attachments leaked onto disk (PNGs and other text blobs from passing tests).
Single markdown table with 5 type rows + Total. Counts shown per type.

Output framed as 'Build warnings by type', counts match `peekie warnings | jq 'group_by(.type)'`. Does not distinguish that 4 'Swift Compiler Error' entries are actually #warning directives (Xcode mis-tags them) — without_skill agent caught this.
Trailing sentence: 'Generic Swift Compiler Warning dominates — worth drilling into those messages first; deprecations are usually the quickest mechanical wins.'
Output is '65.6%' — includes percent sign as the user implied with '%'.
Single decimal place.
Single-line answer.
First line: 'Not all green — 10 test failures in SPM-26.5-iOS.xcresult'.
Numbered markdown table with 10 fully-qualified failing test names.
Failure column on each row contains the assertion or error text.
Single tidy table + 1 sentence of context. No raw JSON dumped. Missed the 2 flaky/mixed tests that with_skill caught.
ls -la confirms /tmp/peekie-eval-failed-attachments-baseline/ contains 7 entries (6 attachment files + manifest.json).

All 6 files belong to the 2 failing tests with attachments (failureWithAttachment() in ExampleSUITests + XCTestTests test_failureWithAttachment()). Agent worked around xcresulttool --only-failures returning empty by enumerating failed test IDs and running per-test exports.
Markdown table maps each file → failing test → repetition + content interpretation.
Directory is clean — only attachments from failing tests are present.
TWO breakdowns: by raw issueType (5 types) and by semantic category (8 categories with example messages). More thorough than with_skill.
Used xcrun xcresulttool get build-results directly. No mention of peekie (expected — baseline has no skill). Achieves correct numeric result but takes longer path.
Interpretation paragraph: '8/17 are intentional #warning(...) TODO markers, not real defects; deprecated-API usage (3) is the largest real category.' Better than with_skill on this point.
Output is '65.6' — bare number. User asked 'single number', strictly speaking this satisfies but is missing the % sign that user used in their request.
Single decimal place.
Single-line answer.
